preFix.title: Composting toilets: what the decision involves
preFix.subtitle: A decision guide for off-grid, cabin, and outbuilding composting toilet systems
preFix.excerpt: A composting toilet processes human waste on-site without water, using aerobic decomposition. It suits off-grid sites, eco-buildings, and outbuildings where mains drainage is unavailable. Understanding the maintenance requirements and regulatory context is essential before installation.
preFix.sourceNotes: Environment Agency guidance on composting toilet compliance. Planning Portal permitted development rules for outbuilding sanitation. BSI PD 6523 guidance on composting toilets. Centre for Alternative Technology composting toilet guidance.

content.text: A composting toilet handles human waste without mains water or a drain connection. It uses aerobic decomposition with a carbon bulking material to reduce waste volume by about 90% over 6 to 12 months. The system suits outbuildings, cabins, and remote sites where drainage installation is impractical. The primary constraints are maintenance requirements, regulatory compliance, and what to do with the processed output.

Self-contained units: the composting chamber is below the seat. Urine is separated and drains to a soakaway or container. Requires ventilation. Suitable for low-use applications. Capacity is limited; requires emptying every 3-6 months depending on use.

Split systems: the toilet seat connects via a chute to a composting chamber below floor or in a separate location. Larger chamber, higher capacity, suits residential use. Requires more installation space and is more complex to empty.
The C:N balance and bulking material

Each use must be followed by a portion of bulking material to maintain the C:N ratio and provide air channels for aerobic decomposition. Without bulking material, the pile compacts, goes anaerobic, and produces foul odours. This is the most common cause of failure in composting toilets in domestic use.

All composting toilets require a continuous ventilation duct from the composting chamber to outside. This draws odours up and out of the chamber. A 110 mm duct of 2 to 4 m vertical height provides passive draw. A small inline fan ensures ventilation even when the building is closed. The duct exit must be away from openable windows and below the ridge to ensure draw rather than backflow.

The processed output is a stable, low-volume material. Most guidance asks for a further 12 months of open-air composting. After that it can be used as a soil conditioner on non-food beds only. UK guidance does not allow it straight on food crops. The Environment Agency classes it as a controlled waste if spread to land.

The urine fraction separated from most modern units is sterile and nitrogen-rich. Diluted at 10:1 with water. It can be used as a liquid plant feed on ornamental plants and established trees. Not on food crops or near water courses.

attrs.body: A composting toilet in an outbuilding or caravan that is not connected to a mains dwelling is usually permitted development. As the main toilet in a habitable building, it may need to meet Building Regulations Part H. Planning policy varies by local authority. Always check with the local planning office before fitting one in a dwelling.
attrs.title: Planning and building regulations

content.text: toilet is not the same as a well-managed
heap in terms of pathogen management. Handle the output with the same hygiene care as raw compost and store away from the dwelling.
